text: The surgeon uses robotic technology to close the abdominal defect, incorporating part of the hernia sac to eliminate the space between the skin and abdominal muscle. Strong, heavy sutures are used to close the defect in two layers, with reduced intra-abdominal pressure to minimize tension on the wound.

The surgeon inserts a two-layer mesh into the abdomen. The rough layer is positioned to attach to the abdominal wall, while the smoother hydrogel layer faces the intra-abdominal structures. The hydrogel layer is absorbable.

The surgeon demonstrates the technique of suturing the edges of the mesh to the abdominal wall, ensuring that no intra-abdominal structures can become trapped between the mesh and the abdominal wall. The mesh is shown being incorporated into the abdominal musculature, forming a sheet over the mesh after two weeks.

The surgeon carefully sutures the mesh to the abdominal wall, ensuring the rough side faces the wall and not the intra-abdominal structures. The mesh features a removable ring in the center, which will be taken out at the end of the procedure. Precise suture placement prevents wrinkling and ensures secure attachment without excessive tension.
The surgeon uses robotic technology to perform a minimally invasive procedure, removing the ring from the mesh through the port after it has separated from the abdominal wall.

The surgeon places additional sutures in the middle of the mesh, securing it to the abdominal wall. The mesh is visibly attached to the muscle without tension or wrinkles, ensuring a smooth and secure fit.
